metadata.title: Write a PRD Like a PM, Part 1: Problem & Solution
metadata.topic: INFO 7375 ASSIGNMENT 2 — PRD PART 1: PROBLEM & SOLUTION (20 PTS)
metadata.playlist: Plan It Like a PM — Your Madison PRD
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 2 brief — Plan Your Madison Project Like a Pro

beats.narration_text: E3. PRD — Product Requirements Document. That is PM-speak for the single written agreement a team makes before anyone writes a line of code. It says: here is the problem we are solving, here is who has it, here is our answer to it, and here is why that answer is worth building. Part one of the PRD is worth 20 points on Assignment 2. It covers two sections: the Problem Statement and the Proposed Solution. Get these right, and every section that follows has a foundation. Get them wrong, and the vagueness compounds.

shot.topic: Episode 3 — PRD Part 1: Problem & Solution (20 pts)
output: Problem Statement (10 pts): specific problem · who has it · cost of not solving it
Proposed Solution (10 pts): your Madison approach · why Madison · what's technically interesting

beats.narration_text: A PRD — Product Requirements Document — is the single written agreement between the team building the product and everyone who asked for it. It answers three questions before engineering starts: what problem does this solve, for whom, and what does success look like. In a real company, a vague problem statement means every engineering decision that follows will drift. Different engineers will interpret the goal differently, and two months in, nobody can agree on whether the product is working. For this assignment, the stakes are the same in miniature: if your problem statement is vague, the rubric deducts from the Problem Statement section, and then every subsequent section — solution, user stories, metrics — inherits the vagueness because they were all built on a weak foundation. The Problem Statement is not an introduction. It is the anchor.

shot.artifactHeading: PRD — what it is and why the problem statement is the anchor
artifactLines: PRD = the written agreement before any code is written
It answers: what problem · for whom · what does success look like
Vague problem statement → every section that follows inherits the vagueness
Problem Statement is not an intro — it is the anchor for the entire document
Part 1 total: 20 pts  |  Problem Statement: 10 pts  |  Proposed Solution: 10 pts

beats.narration_text: Three things are required in a good problem statement. First, WHO: a specific role at a specific type of company — not 'everyone' or 'marketers,' but something like 'a performance marketing manager at a mid-size e-commerce brand.' The more specific the role, the clearer the need. Second, WHAT: the problem itself, stated as something measurable or clearly observable. If you can find an industry number — a study, a report, a benchmark — use it. 'Marketing teams spend an average of 12 hours per week manually scoring ad variants' is a problem. 'Marketing is hard' is not. Third, COST: what happens if nobody solves this. Time lost, errors made, revenue missed, decisions delayed. The assignment uses what it calls the Nina example framing — industry-grounded, real study numbers, not vibes. Here is the prompt to get you started on your own problem statement.

shot.command: I am writing a PRD problem statement for a Madison-powered AI marketing tool. The target user is [ROLE] at [COMPANY TYPE]. The problem they face is [DESCRIBE IN ONE SPECIFIC SENTENCE]. The cost of not solving this is [COST — time/money/errors]. Draft a concise, PM-style problem statement that names the user, the specific pain, and a quantified or observable cost. Then flag any part of the problem that is too broad or unverifiable.
output: WHO: specific role at a specific company type — not 'everyone'
WHAT: measurable or observable pain — use an industry number if one exists
COST: time lost · errors made · revenue missed · decisions delayed

beats.narration_text: Before you finalize the problem statement, run it through three questions. One: is the user a specific role — not 'companies' or 'marketers' in general, but a named function and a named company type? Two: is the pain something you could observe or measure — not just 'it's hard,' but something you could point to in the real world? Three: is there a cost stated — even a rough number or a named downstream effect — if the problem isn't solved? If any answer is no, the statement is too broad, and the rubric will reflect that. Fix it before moving to the solution.

artifactLines: 1. Is the user a specific role (not 'companies' or 'marketers')?       PASS / FAIL
2. Is the pain observable or measurable (not just 'it's hard')?        PASS / FAIL
3. Is there a cost stated — number or named downstream effect?          PASS / FAIL
Any FAIL → statement is too broad → revise before writing the solution
shot.sparkLine: specific user → observable pain → stated cost → PASS

beats.narration_text: Now the Proposed Solution. This section has to do three things to earn full marks. First, name the Madison-powered approach — which layers and components you will use and what they actually do for the problem you just stated. Madison has five layers: Intelligence, Content, Research, Experience, and Performance. Named components include MarketMind Research, Brand Voice Personalization, AI Concierge, and Multi-Armed Bandit optimization using Thompson sampling. Name the ones that are relevant to your project specifically. Second, answer 'why Madison versus alternatives.' What does Madison's agent architecture enable that a generic AI tool or a manual process does not? If you can't answer this, the solution is not specific enough. Third, name what is technically interesting — the part that would require real engineering thinking, not just wiring up an API call. Here is the prompt.

shot.topic: Write a proposed solution for a Madison PRD
shot.command: My problem statement: [PASTE YOUR PROBLEM STATEMENT]. The Madison layer(s) I plan to use: [LAYERS]. Draft a proposed solution paragraph that (1) explains the Madison approach in plain language, (2) answers 'why Madison specifically — what does its agent architecture enable here?', and (3) identifies one technically interesting challenge the solution would need to solve. Keep it to 4–6 sentences.
output: Name the Madison layer(s) and components you'll use — and what they do
Answer: why Madison vs. a generic AI tool or a manual process?
Name one technically interesting challenge — not just an API call

beats.narration_text: Four traps to avoid on Part 1. Trap one: problem too broad. 'Marketing is hard' could describe every marketing product ever built. The problem needs a specific user and a specific pain. Trap two: no cost stated. A problem without a cost is a preference, not a pain worth solving. Trap three: 'why Madison' left unanswered. If you don't say what Madison's architecture specifically enables, your proposed solution is not distinguishable from any generic AI project. Trap four: technical vagueness. 'Use AI to improve marketing' is not technically interesting. 'Use Thompson sampling to allocate budget across A-B variants in real time based on live click-through data' is. Specificity is the entire game in this section.

artifactLines: Trap 1 — Problem too broad: 'marketing is hard' → needs a specific user + specific pain
Trap 2 — No cost stated: a problem without a cost is a preference, not a pain
Trap 3 — 'Why Madison' unanswered: solution indistinguishable from any generic AI project
Trap 4 — Technical vagueness: 'use AI to improve marketing' ≠ technically interesting
Good: 'Thompson sampling to allocate budget across A/B variants in real time'

beats.narration_text: The rules for Part 1. Problem Statement: name a specific user — a role at a company type — state an observable or measurable pain, and give a cost. All three required for full marks. Proposed Solution: name the Madison layer or component, answer why Madison specifically enables something a generic tool does not, and name one technically interesting challenge. Together these two sections are 20 points. And one more thing worth internalizing: if the problem is vague, every section that follows inherits the vagueness. The problem statement is not just worth 10 points. It is the foundation the rest of the PRD stands on.

artifactLines: Problem Statement (10 pts):
· Specific user: a role at a company type — not 'everyone'
· Observable or measurable pain — industry number if available
· Cost stated: time / money / errors / missed revenue
Proposed Solution (10 pts):
· Named Madison layer/component + what it does for this problem
· 'Why Madison' answered — what agent architecture enables specifically
· One technically interesting challenge named
Vague problem → every subsequent section inherits the vagueness

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Take the gap you identified in Episode 2 — specifically the gap your project is built around — and use this prompt to pressure-test your problem statement before you put it on the Figma board. Paste your draft in, run each check, and revise anything that fails. Do not move to user stories until the problem statement passes all four. Claude will tell you exactly which part is too broad or unanswered and will draft an improved version. That is the loop: draft, pressure-test, revise, move on.

shot.segment: Before you finalize — run these checks
shot.command: Here is my draft problem statement: [PASTE YOUR DRAFT]. Run these checks: (1) Is the user specific — a role, not 'companies'? (2) Is the pain observable or measurable? (3) Is there a cost stated? (4) Does the proposed solution answer 'why Madison specifically'? For each check: PASS or FAIL with one sentence explaining why. Then draft an improved version of any section that fails.
output: Check 1: specific user role — PASS / FAIL
Check 2: observable or measurable pain — PASS / FAIL
Check 4: 'why Madison' answered in solution — PASS / FAIL
Improved draft for any failing section
